meta.title: MS-PBI-3: Enterprise Modeling, DAX, and Deployment

questions.stem: Which Power BI visual type is best for showing the distribution of a continuous variable (such as order amounts) and identifying skewness or outliers?

options.text: Clustered bar chart
options.explanation: is incorrect. A clustered bar chart compares discrete categorical values. It shows totals or averages per category and is not designed to reveal the distribution shape of a continuous variable.

options.text: Donut chart
options.explanation: is incorrect. A donut chart shows part-to-whole proportions for a small number of named categories. It cannot represent the continuous distribution or frequency buckets needed for distribution analysis.

options.text: Histogram or box plot visual
options.explanation: is correct. A histogram shows the frequency distribution of a continuous variable across value buckets, making it easy to identify shape, central tendency, spread, and skewness. A box plot shows quartiles, median, and outliers directly. Both are purpose-built for continuous variable distribution analysis.

options.text: Funnel chart
options.explanation: is incorrect. A funnel chart shows sequential stage values typically used for pipeline or conversion analysis, where values decrease from stage to stage. It is not designed to show the distribution of a continuous variable.

questions.reference: [Overview of visualizations in Power BI - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/visuals/power-bi-visualization-types-for-reports-and-q-and-a)

questions.stem: A report page has a bar chart and a table visual. You want to configure the bar chart so that clicking a bar filters the table, but the table does not filter the bar chart when a row is selected. How do you configure this?

options.text: Select the table, go to Format > Edit Interactions, and set the bar chart interaction to None
options.explanation: This only configures the table-to-bar-chart direction; it does not ensure the bar chart filters the table.

options.text: Select the bar chart, go to Format > Edit Interactions, set table to Filter; then select the table and set the bar chart to None
options.explanation: Edit Interactions is set FROM the source visual TO each target. To make the bar chart filter the table: select bar chart, set table to Filter (default). To prevent the table from filtering the bar chart: select table, set bar chart to None. Both interactions need to be configured independently.

options.text: Disable cross-filtering in the report settings
options.explanation: Disabling cross-filtering globally would prevent the bar chart from filtering the table as well, which is the opposite of the desired behavior.

options.text: Select the bar chart, go to Format > Edit Interactions, and set the table interaction to Filter
options.explanation: This only configures one direction (bar chart filters table) but does not prevent the table from also filtering the bar chart.

questions.reference: [Change How Visuals Interact in a Report - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/create-reports/service-reports-visual-interactions)

questions.stem: A data model contains a Sales fact table and a Customers dimension. You need a measure that calculates the average sales per customer, counting only customers who made at least one purchase in the filtered context. Which DAX expression is correct?

options.text: AVERAGE(Sales[Amount])
options.explanation: is incorrect. `AVERAGE(Sales[Amount])` computes the average transaction amount per row, not the average total sales per customer. A customer with multiple transactions would contribute multiple rows, skewing the result.

options.text: DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), COUNTROWS(Customers))
options.explanation: is incorrect. `COUNTROWS(Customers)` counts all customers in the Customers table visible under the current filter, including those who made no purchases. This inflates the denominator and understates the per-purchasing-customer average.

options.text: DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[CustomerID]))
options.explanation: is correct. `DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[CustomerID]))` computes total sales divided by the count of distinct customers who appear in the Sales table within the current filter context — meaning only customers who actually purchased are counted in the denominator.

options.text: AVERAGEX(Customers, SUM(Sales[Amount]))
options.explanation: is incorrect. `AVERAGEX(Customers, SUM(Sales[Amount]))` iterates over all customer rows in context and averages the sales per customer, including customers with no sales who contribute 0 or BLANK. This counts non-purchasing customers and produces a different result from what is required.

questions.stem: You must calculate year-to-date (YTD) total sales in a DAX measure. The fiscal year ends on June 30. Which DAX function correctly handles the non-calendar fiscal year end date?

options.text: CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date], "06/30"))
options.explanation: CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date], "06/30")) uses DATESYTD with the optional year_end_date parameter set to '06/30', which instructs DAX to treat June 30 as the last day of the fiscal year. This produces a YTD calculation that resets on July 1 each year.

options.text: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date], "06-30")
options.explanation: is also technically correct (TOTALYTD with "06-30" uses the same underlying logic), but option A is more explicit in how it constructs the fiscal YTD and is the most commonly recommended form. When two answers appear correct, the question asks for the one that 'correctly handles' the requirement — A is the clearest expression.

options.text: CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date]))
options.explanation: DATESYTD without a year_end_date parameter defaults to December 31, so this would calculate a calendar YTD rather than a fiscal YTD ending June 30.

options.text: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date])
options.explanation: TOTALYTD without a year_end_date parameter also defaults to December 31, not June 30.

questions.stem: You have a many-to-many relationship between Products and Promotions (a product can have multiple promotions; a promotion can apply to multiple products). How should you model this in Power BI?

options.text: Create a direct many-to-many relationship between Products and Promotions using Power BI's built-in many-to-many cardinality
options.explanation: is incorrect. While Power BI does support native many-to-many cardinality between two tables, this approach can produce ambiguous filter propagation and unexpected aggregation results if not carefully managed. The bridge table pattern is preferred for most production scenarios.

options.text: Duplicate the Promotions table and create two separate one-to-many relationships to the duplicated copies
options.explanation: is incorrect. Duplicating the Promotions table creates data redundancy and does not represent the true many-to-many relationship. It also complicates maintenance when promotion data changes.

options.text: Create a bridge table ProductPromotions with ProductID and PromotionID, then create one-to-many relationships from each dimension table to the bridge table
options.explanation: is correct. A bridge (junction) table with one row per product-promotion combination — related to Products and Promotions via one-to-many relationships — implements the many-to-many relationship correctly and gives explicit control over aggregation semantics. This is the preferred approach for clarity and performance.

options.text: Use a CROSSJOIN calculated table to generate all product-promotion combinations and relate it to Sales
options.explanation: is incorrect. A CROSSJOIN calculated table generates the full Cartesian product of both tables — all possible combinations, not just actual product-promotion assignments. This would create incorrect relationships and inaccurate analysis.

questions.reference: [Many-to-many relationships in Power BI Desktop - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-many-to-many-relationships)

questions.stem: You must add a column in Power Query that extracts the domain name from an email address column (e.g., 'user@company.com' → 'company.com'). Which M expression is most readable and purpose-built for this transformation?

options.text: Text.Middle([Email], Text.PositionOf([Email], "@") + 1)
options.explanation: Text.Middle with Text.PositionOf works but requires calculating the position manually, making it more verbose than necessary.

options.text: Text.Split([Email], "@"){1}
options.explanation: Text.Split splits the string into a list and {1} retrieves the second element (the domain). This works but is less readable than Text.AfterDelimiter and requires knowledge of list indexing.

options.text: Text.AfterDelimiter([Email], "@")
options.explanation: Text.AfterDelimiter is the Power Query function specifically designed to extract text after a delimiter character. It is the most readable and semantically clear approach for this transformation, and it handles edge cases like multiple '@' symbols by defaulting to the first occurrence.

options.text: Text.Select([Email], {"a".."z", "."})
options.explanation: Text.Select filters characters from a set, which would remove the '@' but would also remove digits and hyphens that are valid in domain names (e.g., 'company-1.com' would lose the '1').

questions.stem: A CALCULATE expression uses REMOVEFILTERS(DimDate[Year]) as a filter modifier. What does REMOVEFILTERS do in this context compared to ALL(DimDate[Year])?

options.text: REMOVEFILTERS removes only external slicer filters; ALL also removes filters coming from related tables through relationships
options.explanation: is incorrect. Both `REMOVEFILTERS` and `ALL` as CALCULATE modifiers remove all active filters on the specified column regardless of their origin (slicer, page filter, visual filter, or relationship). There is no distinction by filter source.

options.text: REMOVEFILTERS cannot accept a column argument; it only accepts a full table as its argument
options.explanation: is incorrect. `REMOVEFILTERS` accepts both table and column arguments, just like `ALL`. `REMOVEFILTERS(DimDate[Year])` with a column argument is perfectly valid.

options.text: REMOVEFILTERS is a semantic alias for ALL when used as a CALCULATE modifier; they produce identical results in this context
options.explanation: is correct. `REMOVEFILTERS` was introduced as a clearer semantic alias for `ALL` when used as a CALCULATE filter modifier. `REMOVEFILTERS(DimDate[Year])` and `ALL(DimDate[Year])` inside CALCULATE produce identical results: both remove any active filter on `DimDate[Year]` from the evaluation context. The `REMOVEFILTERS` name makes the intent explicit — remove filters — rather than returning all values, which is what `ALL` does in a table context.

options.text: REMOVEFILTERS returns an empty table, while ALL returns all rows; only ALL can be used inside CALCULATE as a filter argument
options.explanation: is incorrect. `REMOVEFILTERS` does not return an empty table. As a CALCULATE modifier it removes the filter context, not the data. It is valid inside CALCULATE and produces the same behavior as `ALL`.

questions.stem: A report page has a slicer for [Year] and a line chart showing monthly sales. You want the chart to show all years' data as a comparison regardless of the Year slicer selection. Which action should you do?

options.text: Set the visual-level filter on the line chart to exclude the Year field
options.explanation: Setting a visual-level filter to exclude Year would hide all data filtered by Year but would not allow all years to appear simultaneously.

options.text: Create a measure using ALL(Dates[Year]) to remove the year filter context
options.explanation: Creating a measure with ALL(Dates[Year]) removes the Year filter from the measure's computation, but other visuals on the page would still be filtered by the Year slicer.

options.text: Duplicate the page and remove the slicer from the duplicate
options.explanation: Duplicating the page and removing the slicer is a workaround that creates maintenance overhead; Edit Interactions is the correct purpose-built approach.

options.text: Use Edit interactions from the slicer to set the interaction with the line chart to 'None'
options.explanation: Power BI's Edit Interactions feature allows you to control whether a slicer or visual filters another visual. Setting the interaction from the Year slicer to the line chart to 'None' (the crossed-out icon) prevents the slicer from filtering that visual. Edit Interactions is accessed by selecting the slicer and clicking Format > Edit interactions.

questions.reference: [Change How Visuals Interact in a Report - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/create-reports/service-reports-visual-interactions)

questions.stem: You connect to a SQL Server database in Power Query. The query retrieves 2 million rows but only 100,000 are needed after filtering. You want to reduce data transfer and improve refresh performance. What should you do?

options.text: Import all rows and use a calculated column with IF to exclude unnecessary rows after the fact
options.explanation: is incorrect. A calculated column in the model evaluates after all data is imported. This reduces model size but does not reduce the volume of data transferred during refresh, which is the stated goal.

options.text: Apply a filter step in Power Query after a non-foldable transformation step so the filter runs in the Power Query engine
options.explanation: is incorrect. Applying a filter after a non-foldable step breaks the fold chain. The filter executes in Power Query's in-memory engine rather than being pushed to SQL Server, so all 2 million rows are still transferred.

options.text: Enable query folding by applying the filter step immediately after the source step so the filter is pushed to SQL Server
options.explanation: is correct. Query folding translates Power Query transformation steps into native SQL operations executed on the source. Applying the filter step directly after the Source step maximizes the chance of folding, pushing the WHERE clause to SQL Server and reducing data transferred across the network.

options.text: Switch the dataset to DirectQuery mode so no data is ever imported into Power BI
options.explanation: is incorrect. DirectQuery avoids caching but does not eliminate data transfer — it issues live SQL queries on each visual interaction, which can be slower for complex reports and does not reduce query load on the source server.

questions.stem: You must display sales by country on a map visual. Countries are stored as two-letter ISO codes (e.g., 'US', 'GB'). The map shows incorrect locations for some countries. Which action should you do?

options.text: Replace ISO codes with full country names
options.explanation: Replacing ISO codes with full country names is unnecessary — Power BI's geocoding engine supports ISO country codes natively once the data category is set correctly.

options.text: Use a filled map instead of a bubble map
options.explanation: Switching between filled map and bubble map changes the visual type but does not affect geocoding accuracy; the underlying geocoding issue remains.

options.text: Set the Data Category of the country column to 'Country or Region' in the column properties
options.explanation: Power BI uses data category metadata to interpret geographic data correctly. Setting the Data Category to 'Country or Region' (via Column Tools tab in Power BI Desktop) helps the map visual and Bing geocoder understand that the column contains country identifiers. Without this, Power BI may misinterpret 'US' as a US state abbreviation or city name.

options.text: Enable the 'Use Microsoft Bing geocoding' option in report settings
options.explanation: Microsoft Bing geocoding is used by default for map visuals; enabling it explicitly is not an available report setting that would fix geocoding.

questions.reference: [Data categorization in Power BI Desktop - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-data-categorization)

questions.stem: You want report consumers to be able to drill through from a summary sales page to a detailed transactions page filtered by the selected product. How do you configure this?

options.text: Create a bookmark for the detail page and link it from a button on the summary visual
options.explanation: is incorrect. Bookmarks capture a static report state and can navigate to a page, but they do not pass the selected data context as a filter. The detail page would not be filtered to the product the user clicked.

options.text: Add the Product field to the Drill through well of the detail page in the Visualizations pane
options.explanation: is correct. Power BI drill-through is configured on the destination (detail) page by adding the drill-through field to the Drill through well in the Visualizations pane. Users right-click a data point on the source page and select 'Drill through > [detail page]', and Power BI navigates to the detail page with the selected product as the filter context.

options.text: Enable Page Navigation in the summary visual's Format pane and set the target page
options.explanation: is incorrect. Page Navigation buttons navigate to a fixed page without passing any filter context from the source visual. The detail page would not be filtered by the selected product.

options.text: Use Sync Slicers to synchronize the product slicer across the summary and detail pages
options.explanation: is incorrect. Sync Slicers synchronize the same slicer's selection across multiple pages, which requires the user to manually change a slicer. It does not enable right-click drill-through from a chart data point.

questions.reference: [Drillthrough in Power BI Reports: Navigate to Detailed Insights - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/create-reports/desktop-drillthrough)

questions.stem: You use a deployment pipeline (Dev → Test → Production) in Power BI. After deploying to Production, you discover that dataset connection strings still point to the Test database. Which action should you configure to fix this for future deployments?

options.text: Configure deployment pipeline rules to map parameters or data source connection details between stages
options.explanation: Power BI deployment pipelines support deployment rules that automatically transform dataset properties (data source connection strings, Power Query parameter values) when deploying between stages. Rules are configured per pipeline stage and apply during each deployment, eliminating manual post-deployment updates.

options.text: Use Power Query parameters set to the Test environment values in all stages
options.explanation: Parameters set to Test environment values would still point to Test after deployment unless a rule remaps them to Production values.

options.text: Create separate PBIX files for each environment
options.explanation: Maintaining separate PBIX files per environment creates duplication and makes version management difficult; deployment pipeline rules are the purpose-built solution.

options.text: Manually update connection strings after each deployment in Production
options.explanation: Manually updating connection strings after each deployment is error-prone and does not scale.

questions.stem: You have a Power BI report in a Premium workspace. You want to give external users from a partner organization read-only access to the report without creating Microsoft 365 accounts for them. What feature enables this?

options.text: Power BI guest access via Azure AD B2B
options.explanation: is correct. Azure AD B2B (Business-to-Business) allows external users from a partner organization to access Power BI content using their own organizational identity. Administrators invite external users via Azure AD, and they authenticate with their existing credentials without needing a Microsoft 365 account in your tenant.

options.text: Publish to web to create a publicly accessible anonymous URL
options.explanation: is incorrect. Publish to web creates a publicly accessible, completely unauthenticated URL — anyone with the link can view the report. This is not appropriate for partner access where access control is required.

options.text: Embed the report in a SharePoint Online page shared with the partner
options.explanation: is incorrect. Embedding in SharePoint Online requires the users to have Microsoft 365 accounts in a tenant that has access to the SharePoint site. External partners without Microsoft 365 accounts cannot use this approach.

options.text: Export the report to PDF and email it to the partner contacts
options.explanation: is incorrect. Exporting to PDF creates a static snapshot that cannot be interacted with, filtered, or kept up to date. It does not provide the controlled, self-service read-only report access the scenario requires.

questions.stem: Which Power BI storage mode allows data to be cached in the Power BI service for fast query performance while also allowing live queries to the source for the most recent data?

options.text: DirectQuery mode
options.explanation: DirectQuery always queries the source live and never caches data locally; there is no cached copy.

options.text: Dual (Mixed) mode
options.explanation: Dual mode (also called Mixed or Dual storage mode) allows a table to behave as either Import or DirectQuery depending on the query context. When used with dimension tables alongside DirectQuery fact tables, the engine queries cached data for dimensions (fast) and live data for facts (current).

options.text: Live connection to Analysis Services
options.explanation: A live connection to Analysis Services queries the remote model on every request; it does not cache data in the Power BI service.

options.text: Import mode
options.explanation: Import mode caches data locally but never queries the source live; data is only updated during scheduled or manual refreshes.

questions.reference: [Table Storage Mode in Power BI Semantic Models - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-storage-mode)

questions.stem: You need to prevent report authors in other workspaces from building reports that live-connect to a dataset in your workspace. Which setting enforces this?

options.text: Enable 'Block republish and disable package refresh' in the workspace settings
options.explanation: is incorrect. 'Block republish and disable package refresh' is not a standard Power BI workspace setting name. This description does not match any built-in control for live connection restrictions.

options.text: Enable 'Discourage live connections from reports on other workspaces' in the dataset settings
options.explanation: is correct. Power BI dataset settings include 'Discourage live connections from reports on other workspaces', which prevents report authors in other workspaces from creating live connections (thin reports) against this dataset. This enforces colocation of reports and datasets in the same workspace.

options.text: Remove the Build permission from all users for the dataset
options.explanation: is incorrect. Removing the Build permission prevents all external connections including ones that might be legitimate. The 'Discourage live connections' setting is a more targeted control that discourages cross-workspace usage while still allowing authorized builds.

options.text: Enable XMLA endpoint restriction to limit connections to Premium-only clients
options.explanation: is incorrect. XMLA endpoint restriction controls which clients (third-party tools like Excel or Tabular Editor) can connect via the XMLA protocol. It does not govern Power BI Service report live connections.

questions.reference: [Build Permission for Shared Semantic Models - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/connect-data/service-datasets-build-permissions)

questions.stem: A Q&A visual is not recognizing the synonym 'revenue' for the measure named 'Total Sales'. How do you fix this?

options.text: Rename the measure to 'Revenue' in the model
options.explanation: is incorrect. Renaming the measure to 'Revenue' would break all existing report visuals, calculated columns, and other measures that reference [Total Sales] by its current name — an unnecessarily disruptive change for a synonym need.

options.text: Add 'revenue' as a synonym in the Q&A Synonyms pane or linguistic schema for the [Total Sales] measure
options.explanation: is correct. Power BI Q&A supports custom synonyms defined in the Synonyms pane (accessible via the Modeling tab or Q&A setup). Adding 'revenue' as a synonym for [Total Sales] allows users to query with either term. The linguistic schema can also be edited directly via the Q&A setup for more advanced customization.

options.text: Create a second measure named 'Revenue' that references [Total Sales]
options.explanation: is incorrect. Creating a duplicate measure named 'Revenue' pollutes the model with redundant measures and requires maintenance if [Total Sales] logic changes. Adding a synonym is the correct and maintainable approach.

options.text: Enable the Q&A natural language training feature in the Power BI tenant admin settings
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no 'Q&A natural language training' toggle in Power BI tenant admin settings. Synonyms are defined at the model level, not the tenant level.

questions.reference: [Intro to Q&A tooling to train Power BI Q&A (preview) - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/natural-language/q-and-a-tooling-intro)

questions.stem: You use a deployment pipeline (Dev → Test → Production) in Power BI. After deploying to Production, you discover that dataset connection strings still point to the Test database. What should you configure to fix this for future deployments?

options.text: Manually update connection strings after each deployment in the Production dataset settings
options.explanation: is incorrect. Manually updating connection strings after each deployment is error-prone and time-consuming. Deployment rules exist specifically to automate this process and eliminate the risk of forgetting to update the Production connection.

options.text: Configure deployment pipeline rules to map data source parameters or connection details between stages
options.explanation: is correct. Power BI deployment pipelines support deployment rules that automatically transform dataset properties — including data source connection strings and Power Query parameter values — when deploying between stages. Rules are configured per pipeline stage and apply on each deployment, eliminating manual post-deployment updates.

options.text: Create separate PBIX files for each environment and deploy them independently
options.explanation: is incorrect. Using separate PBIX files per environment defeats the purpose of a deployment pipeline. It creates maintenance overhead and the risk of the environments diverging in structure or logic.

options.text: Keep Power Query parameters set to Test values in all stages and change them manually after each deploy
options.explanation: is incorrect. Setting parameters to Test values everywhere and updating manually is the same problem as option A — it requires a manual step that can be forgotten, and it is the problem the scenario is asking to solve.

questions.reference: [Get started using deployment pipelines, the Fabric Application lifecycle management (ALM) tool - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/create-reports/deployment-pipelines-get-started)

questions.stem: A report page has a slicer for [Year] and a line chart showing monthly sales. You want the chart to show all years' data as a comparison regardless of the Year slicer selection. What should you do?

options.text: Set a visual-level filter on the line chart to exclude the Year field
options.explanation: is incorrect. Visual-level filters on a visual do not prevent a slicer from filtering it. Visual-level filters add additional constraints on top of any slicer context that is already applied.

options.text: Duplicate the page and remove the slicer from the duplicate for the comparison view
options.explanation: is incorrect. Duplicating the page creates a separate page without the slicer, but this requires users to navigate between pages for the comparison. The single-page solution using Edit Interactions is simpler and more user-friendly.

options.text: Create a measure using ALL(Dates[Year]) to remove the year filter context inside the measure
options.explanation: is incorrect. A measure using `ALL(Dates[Year])` would remove the year filter inside the measure's calculation, but the visual's own axis and category groupings would still be affected by the slicer, potentially showing only the selected year's months on the axis.

options.text: Use Edit Interactions from the slicer and set the interaction with the line chart to None
options.explanation: is correct. Power BI's Edit Interactions feature allows control over whether a slicer or visual filters another visual. Setting the interaction from the Year slicer to the line chart to 'None' (the crossed-out icon) disconnects the slicer from that specific visual without affecting other visuals on the page.

questions.reference: [Change How Visuals Interact in a Report - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/create-reports/service-reports-visual-interactions)

questions.stem: In a Power BI data model, the relationship between a Dates table and a Sales fact table is a one-to-many relationship with single-directional cross-filter. An analyst reports that a slicer on the Dates table does not filter a separate Products dimension table. What should you do?

options.text: Change the Dates-to-Sales relationship to bidirectional cross-filter
options.explanation: is incorrect. Enabling bidirectional on Dates-to-Sales allows Sales to filter back into Dates, which can cause ambiguous filter paths and incorrect aggregations in many reports. It does not help Products receive the filter.

options.text: Create a direct relationship between the Dates table and the Products table
options.explanation: is incorrect. Adding a direct Dates-to-Products relationship would create an ambiguous relationship path alongside Dates → Sales → Products, which Power BI would warn about or disable. It is not the right architectural fix.

options.text: Enable bidirectional cross-filter on the Sales-to-Products relationship
options.explanation: is correct. Filters flow from dimension tables to fact tables in single-direction mode. For a Dates slicer to filter Products, the filter must propagate: Dates → Sales → Products. Enabling bidirectional cross-filter on the Sales-to-Products relationship allows the filter to pass through Sales to Products.

options.text: Add a CROSSFILTER DAX measure that links Dates directly to Products at query time
options.explanation: is incorrect. CROSSFILTER is a DAX function used within a CALCULATE expression to modify filter direction for a specific measure. It does not create a persistent slicer-filtering behavior across the whole report.

questions.reference: [Model relationships in Power BI Desktop - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-relationships-understand)

questions.stem: A dataset refreshes from an on-premises SQL Server database. The refresh fails with 'Unable to connect to the data source'. The on-premises data gateway is installed and running. What should you check first?

options.text: Verify that the gateway machine has outbound access to Power BI service endpoints on port 443
options.explanation: is correct. The on-premises data gateway communicates with Power BI service via outbound HTTPS on port 443 and optionally Azure Service Bus on ports 5671 and 5672. When the gateway is running but refresh fails with a connection error, the most common cause is a firewall blocking outbound connectivity from the gateway machine to Power BI service endpoints. Checking firewall and network rules is the first diagnostic step.

options.text: Reinstall Power BI Desktop on the gateway machine to refresh its connection cache
options.explanation: is incorrect. Power BI Desktop is a client authoring tool and is not required on the gateway machine. The gateway service runs independently of Desktop. Reinstalling Desktop would not affect gateway connectivity.

options.text: Switch the dataset from Import to DirectQuery mode to bypass the gateway
options.explanation: is incorrect. Switching to DirectQuery still requires the gateway for on-premises sources. The gateway is not bypassed by DirectQuery — both Import and DirectQuery modes use the gateway for on-premises data sources.

options.text: Upgrade the workspace to Premium capacity to enable on-premises gateway refresh
options.explanation: is incorrect. Premium capacity is not required for on-premises data gateway refresh. Pro workspaces support scheduled refresh via the gateway.

questions.stem: You need to calculate year-to-date (YTD) total sales in a DAX measure. The fiscal year ends on June 30. Which DAX expression is correct?

options.text: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date])
options.explanation: is incorrect. `TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date])` uses the default calendar year end of December 31. For a fiscal year ending June 30, this produces the wrong date range and returns incorrect results.

options.text: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date], "06-30")
options.explanation: is correct. `TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date], "06-30")` uses the optional year-end date parameter to set the fiscal year boundary to June 30. Without this parameter, DAX defaults to December 31, producing a calendar-year YTD rather than a fiscal-year YTD.

options.text: CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date]))
options.explanation: is incorrect. `DATESYTD(Dates[Date])` without a year_end_date argument also defaults to December 31. The fiscal year boundary is not respected.

options.text: CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESBETWEEN(Dates[Date], STARTOFYEAR(Dates[Date]), TODAY()))
options.explanation: is incorrect. `DATESBETWEEN` with `STARTOFYEAR` and `TODAY()` uses the calendar year start (January 1) and the current date. It does not account for a June 30 fiscal year end and would produce the wrong cumulative range.

questions.stem: A workspace contains reports used by 500 concurrent users. You need to ensure the workspace can handle concurrent query load without performance throttling. Which Power BI license or capacity model is most appropriate?

options.text: 500 individual Power BI Pro licenses assigned to each user
options.explanation: is incorrect. 500 Pro licenses allow users to publish and share content but all Pro workloads share the same capacity pool. Under high concurrent load, Pro workspaces can be throttled as they compete for shared resources.

options.text: Power BI Premium Per Capacity (P-SKU) assigned to the workspace
options.explanation: is correct. Power BI Premium Per Capacity (P-SKU) assigns dedicated compute capacity to a workspace, providing isolated resources scaled to the SKU size. This handles high concurrent query loads without the throttling that affects shared infrastructure. Premium capacity also allows consumers to view reports without Pro licenses, reducing per-user cost for large audiences.

options.text: Power BI Premium Per User (PPU) licenses for all 500 users
options.explanation: is incorrect. Premium Per User (PPU) provides Premium features per user but runs on shared infrastructure rather than dedicated capacity. For 500 concurrent users with performance SLAs, dedicated capacity is more appropriate.

options.text: Power BI Embedded (A-SKU) for internal user distribution
options.explanation: is incorrect. Power BI Embedded (A-SKU) is designed for ISVs embedding Power BI into customer-facing applications. It is not intended for internal organizational user distribution and requires additional application development.

questions.stem: A measure [Running Total] = CALCULATE([Total Sales], FILTER(ALL(Dates[Date]), Dates[Date] <= MAX(Dates[Date]))) works correctly in a table sorted by date but shows the same value in every column of a matrix with months as column headers. What is the issue?

options.text: The matrix column context does not generate a row context; use EARLIER() to access the column's date value
options.explanation: is incorrect. `EARLIER()` is used for row context transitions in calculated columns or nested FILTER scenarios. The issue here is filter context — specifically that `ALL` removes the column filter — not a row context problem.

options.text: MAX(Dates[Date]) in the matrix column context returns the max date for that column's month, which is correct
options.explanation: is incorrect. Inside the FILTER iteration, `MAX(Dates[Date])` does not evaluate in the column's filter context because `ALL(Dates[Date])` has already removed that context. It evaluates over all dates, not just the column's month.

options.text: ALL(Dates[Date]) removes the matrix column filter, so MAX(Dates[Date]) evaluates to the overall maximum date; every column's threshold is the same, producing the same grand total
options.explanation: is correct. `ALL(Dates[Date])` removes all filters on `Dates[Date]`, including the filter that the matrix column header (month) places on the date column. Without a column filter, `MAX(Dates[Date])` inside the FILTER evaluates over the full unfiltered date table and returns the overall maximum date. Since every column uses the same maximum date as its upper bound, every column shows the same cumulative total (the grand total).

options.text: Running totals are only supported in table and line chart visuals; matrix visuals do not support them
options.explanation: is incorrect. Matrix visuals fully support running total measures. The issue is a DAX pattern problem, not a visual type limitation.

questions.reference: [Avoid using FILTER as a filter argument in DAX - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/best-practices/dax-avoid-avoid-filter-as-filter-argument)

questions.stem: You must implement row-level security (RLS) so that each regional manager can only see data for their own region. The mapping of managers to regions is in a separate table 'ManagerRegions'. What is the correct setup?

options.text: Use Object Level Security to hide the Region column from non-admin users
options.explanation: Object Level Security hides columns or tables from users but does not filter row data; managers could still see all regions if they accessed the data through another route.

options.text: Create an RLS role with a DAX filter: [Region] = USERPRINCIPALNAME()
options.explanation: Applying USERPRINCIPALNAME() directly on [Region] only works if regions are named with email addresses, which is not the case here.

options.text: Create a calculated column in the fact table using USERPRINCIPALNAME() and filter on it
options.explanation: USERPRINCIPALNAME() returns a value at query time based on the current user; it cannot be used in a calculated column, which is computed at data load time without a user context.

options.text: Create an RLS role that filters the ManagerRegions table: [ManagerEmail] = USERPRINCIPALNAME(), ensure ManagerRegions relates to the Region dimension
options.explanation: Dynamic RLS using USERPRINCIPALNAME() should be applied on the ManagerRegions mapping table, which relates to the Region dimension. The DAX filter [ManagerEmail] = USERPRINCIPALNAME() restricts the ManagerRegions table to the logged-in user's row(s), which cascades through the relationship to filter the Region dimension and ultimately the fact table.

questions.stem: A measure [Sales LY] uses CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dates[Date])). When the report is filtered to a single month (March 2024), [Sales LY] returns March 2023 correctly. But when filtered to a custom date range (Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2024), [Sales LY] returns an incorrect value. Why?

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR requires a contiguous date range; partial month ranges are not supported
options.explanation: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR works with any set of dates, including partial months and custom ranges; it does not require a contiguous or full-period range.

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR only works with standard calendar periods (month, quarter, year); use DATEADD(Dates[Date], -1, YEAR) for custom ranges
options.explanation: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR works on any set of dates, not only standard calendar periods. DATEADD is an alternative but SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR is not broken here.

options.text: The Dates table is missing a row for Feb 15, 2023, causing the calculation to return null
options.explanation: A missing date row would cause that specific date to return null, not the entire range; also, the Dates table should contain all dates in the model range.

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR shifts any set of dates back by one year; it works correctly — the user's expectation is wrong
options.explanation: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR returns the same set of dates shifted back exactly one year. For Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2024, it returns Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2023. This is mathematically correct. If the user expects something different (e.g., full year or specific business period), SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR is working as designed. The 'incorrect value' reflects the user's expectation, not a bug.

questions.stem: You have two queries: 'Orders' and 'Returns'. You need to combine them to see all orders with a flag indicating whether each was returned. Some orders have no corresponding return record. Which Power Query merge join type should you use?

options.text: Inner join
options.explanation: is incorrect. Inner join returns only rows with a match in both tables. Orders that were never returned would be excluded entirely, which is not the desired result.

options.text: Full outer join
options.explanation: is incorrect. Full outer join includes all rows from both tables, which would also include return records with no matching order — adding spurious rows to the result.

options.text: Left outer join
options.explanation: is correct. A Left outer join keeps all rows from the left table (Orders) and brings in matching rows from the right table (Returns). Orders with no return get null in the Returns columns, which can be used as the returned flag. This preserves every order regardless of return status.

options.text: Right anti join
options.explanation: is incorrect. Right anti join returns only rows from the right table (Returns) that have no match in the left table. This is the inverse of what is needed and would exclude all matched orders.

questions.stem: You create a relationship between FactSales[ProductID] (many side) and DimProduct[ProductID] (one side). The relationship is valid in Power BI. What must be true about DimProduct[ProductID]?

options.text: It must contain unique, non-null values to serve as the one side of the relationship
options.explanation: is correct. The 'one' side of a one-to-many relationship in Power BI must have unique, non-null values. If DimProduct[ProductID] contains duplicates or blanks, Power BI cannot create a valid 1:many relationship and would classify it as many:many.

options.text: It must be an integer data type because Power BI does not support text relationship keys
options.explanation: is incorrect. Power BI relationship keys can be any comparable data type including text, integer, date, or GUID. The types on both sides must be compatible, but there is no requirement for integers.

options.text: It must contain every ProductID value that appears in FactSales, with no gaps
options.explanation: is incorrect. The one side does not need to cover every key value in the fact table. Fact table rows with ProductIDs not found in DimProduct simply have no matching dimension row and may return blank for dimension attributes — a referential integrity gap, not a relationship creation error.

options.text: It must be explicitly set as the table's key column in the model view properties
options.explanation: is incorrect. Power BI does not require a formal 'key column' designation in model view properties to create a relationship. The uniqueness constraint is enforced by Power BI during relationship creation, not by a column property.

questions.reference: [Model relationships in Power BI Desktop - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-relationships-understand)

questions.stem: A Power Query table has a column named [Sales Amount] that contains text values like '$1,234.56'. You need to convert it to a decimal number. What is the correct sequence of transformations?

options.text: Change the column data type directly to Decimal Number
options.explanation: is incorrect. Changing the type directly fails at runtime because Power Query cannot parse '$1,234.56' as a number. The result is error rows rather than numeric values.

options.text: Use Replace Values to remove '$' and ',', then change data type to Decimal Number
options.explanation: is correct. Directly changing a text column with currency symbols and thousand separators to Decimal Number causes conversion errors because the dollar sign and comma are not valid decimal characters. The correct sequence is to first clean the text — Replace Values to remove '$', then Replace Values to remove ',' — and finally change the data type to Decimal Number.

options.text: Add a custom column using Number.FromText([Sales Amount]) without cleaning first
options.explanation: is incorrect. `Number.FromText` also fails on strings containing '$' and ','. It requires a clean numeric string such as '1234.56' to succeed.

options.text: Use Text.ToNumber in a new column to convert the raw currency string
options.explanation: is incorrect. `Text.ToNumber` is not a built-in Power Query M function. The correct function is `Number.FromText`, and even that requires the currency symbols to be removed first.

questions.stem: A Power Query step was written to reference a column named 'Revenue'. A subsequent step renames that column to 'Total Revenue'. What happens when the query is refreshed?

options.text: Power Query automatically updates the earlier step to reference 'Total Revenue' when it detects the rename
options.explanation: is incorrect. Power Query does not automatically update references in earlier or later steps when a column is renamed. Each step's M code is static until manually edited.

options.text: Power Query does not support renaming columns that are referenced in formula steps
options.explanation: is incorrect. Power Query allows renaming columns that are referenced in other steps; it simply does not automatically cascade the name change to dependent steps. Those steps must be manually updated.

options.text: Steps evaluated after the rename step will fail because they can no longer find a column named 'Revenue'
options.explanation: is correct. Power Query evaluates steps sequentially. When a rename step changes 'Revenue' to 'Total Revenue', any later step that still references the original name 'Revenue' will fail with a column not found error at refresh time, because 'Revenue' no longer exists after the rename step.

options.text: Power Query formulas use column index positions, so renaming a column does not affect any steps
options.explanation: is incorrect. Power Query M formula steps reference columns by name, not by index position. Renaming a column breaks any downstream step that uses the old name by string reference.

questions.stem: You need to add a column in Power Query that extracts the domain name from an email address column (e.g., 'user@company.com' → 'company.com'). Which M expression is the most readable and idiomatic approach?

options.text: Text.AfterDelimiter([Email], "@")
options.explanation: is correct. `Text.AfterDelimiter([Email], "@")` returns everything after the first '@' character — the domain portion — in a single, readable function call. It is the most idiomatic and concise M expression for this task.

options.text: Text.Middle([Email], Text.Length([Email]) - Text.Length(Text.AfterDelimiter([Email], "@")))
options.explanation: is incorrect. This expression computes the domain length indirectly and uses `Text.Middle` to extract it. While it could produce the domain, the logic is unnecessarily convoluted and harder to read and maintain than `Text.AfterDelimiter`.

options.text: Text.Split([Email], "@"){0}
options.explanation: is incorrect. `Text.Split([Email], "@"){0}` returns index 0 — the part before the '@' — which is the username, not the domain. The domain is at index {1}.

options.text: Text.Range([Email], 0, Text.PositionOf([Email], "@"))
options.explanation: is incorrect. `Text.Range([Email], 0, Text.PositionOf([Email], "@"))` extracts characters from the start up to the '@', returning the username. To get the domain, you would need everything after the '@' position, which is what `Text.AfterDelimiter` does more cleanly.

questions.stem: A report uses a measure [Profit Margin %] = DIVIDE([Profit], [Revenue]). When added to a matrix with subtotals, the subtotal rows show incorrect margin percentages (they appear to be averaging the row percentages). What is the root cause?

options.text: The measure is working correctly; the user misunderstands margin aggregation
options.explanation: While DAX does re-evaluate measures in subtotal context, the symptom described (appearing to average row percentages) indicates the underlying measures are aggregating incorrectly — not that the user is wrong.

options.text: Use SUMX over the matrix rows to produce correct subtotals
options.explanation: SUMX iterates rows and would not produce the correct subtotal margin either; the issue is with how the underlying Profit and Revenue measures aggregate.

options.text: DIVIDE averages its inputs at aggregation levels by default; use SUM instead
options.explanation: DIVIDE does not average its inputs; it divides them. If the result looks like an average of child rows, the numerator or denominator measures are aggregating incorrectly.

options.text: Matrix subtotals apply SUM aggregation to the measure values of child rows instead of re-evaluating the measure in the subtotal's filter context
options.explanation: When a matrix shows subtotals, Power BI re-evaluates the DAX measure at the subtotal's filter context (the combined filter of the subtotal group). If subtotals look wrong, the issue is typically that [Profit] or [Revenue] measures have incorrect aggregation at the subtotal level (e.g., using AVERAGE instead of SUM), causing the ratio to appear as an average of child row percentages rather than the correctly computed ratio.

questions.reference: [Avoid using FILTER as a filter argument in DAX - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/guidance/dax-avoid-avoid-filter-as-filter-argument)

questions.stem: A report uses a measure [Profit Margin %] = DIVIDE([Profit], [Revenue]). When added to a matrix with subtotals, the subtotal rows show percentages that appear to average the child row percentages rather than recalculate from totals. What is the most likely root cause?

options.text: DIVIDE averages its inputs at aggregation levels by default; replace it with a ratio formula using SUM
options.explanation: is incorrect. `DIVIDE` does not average its inputs. It simply divides numerator by denominator in whatever filter context it is evaluated. The function itself is not the source of incorrect aggregation behavior.

options.text: The [Profit] or [Revenue] base measures use AVERAGE or AVERAGEX instead of SUM, so the subtotal aggregates averages rather than sums the component values
options.explanation: is correct. `DIVIDE([Profit], [Revenue])` at the subtotal level re-evaluates both [Profit] and [Revenue] in the subtotal's filter context and then divides. If [Profit] or [Revenue] internally uses `AVERAGE` or `AVERAGEX` instead of `SUM`, the subtotal computes the average of amounts rather than the total, producing margin values that look like they are averaging child row percentages.

options.text: The measure is working correctly; the user misunderstands how margin aggregation works across categories
options.explanation: is incorrect. The measure is likely not working correctly. DAX measures re-evaluate at each level of the matrix in that level's filter context — if the base measures are correct, subtotal margins should reflect the correct total-level ratio.

options.text: Matrix subtotals apply SUM to the child measure values rather than re-evaluating the measure in the subtotal filter context
options.explanation: is incorrect. DAX measures are not aggregated by SUM across child rows. Each matrix cell — including subtotal cells — triggers a fresh DAX evaluation in the cell's own filter context. Power BI does not sum up measure values from child rows to produce subtotals.

questions.reference: [Avoid using FILTER as a filter argument in DAX - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/best-practices/dax-avoid-avoid-filter-as-filter-argument)

questions.stem: Your organization wants to automate Power BI dataset refresh using the REST API from an Azure Data Factory pipeline after ETL loads new data. Which authentication method is recommended for service-to-service API calls?

options.text: Service principal with workspace role assignment and Power BI API permission enabled in tenant settings
options.explanation: is correct. Service principals (Azure AD application registrations) are the Microsoft-recommended authentication method for service-to-service Power BI REST API automation. They use certificate or client secret credentials rather than user passwords, support role-based access via workspace roles, and require the 'Allow service principals to use Power BI APIs' setting to be enabled in the Power BI tenant admin portal.

options.text: A shared user account with a Power BI Pro license whose credentials are stored in Azure Key Vault
options.explanation: is incorrect. Using a shared user account with stored credentials is a security anti-pattern. If the account password changes or the account is disabled, all automation breaks. Service accounts also consume a Pro license unnecessarily.

options.text: A master user account using the OAuth resource owner password credential (ROPC) flow with username and password
options.explanation: is incorrect. The OAuth Resource Owner Password Credential (ROPC) flow transmits the user's password directly and is deprecated by Microsoft for new applications. It is not MFA-compatible and is considered insecure for production automation.

options.text: An API key generated in Power BI service tenant settings
options.explanation: is incorrect. Power BI service does not have an API key generation feature in tenant settings. Authentication to the Power BI REST API always uses OAuth 2.0 tokens obtained via Azure AD.

questions.stem: You must unpivot a table where months are column headers (Jan, Feb, Mar...) and product names are row headers. The result should have columns: Product, Month, Sales. Which Power Query transformation achieves this most robustly as new month columns are added?

options.text: Select the Product column, then choose 'Unpivot Other Columns'
options.explanation: 'Unpivot Other Columns' (selecting the Product column and choosing Unpivot Other Columns) unpivots everything except the selected column. This approach is more robust than selecting individual month columns because it automatically includes any new month columns added to the source data without requiring query changes.

options.text: Select all month columns individually, then choose 'Unpivot Columns'
options.explanation: Explicitly selecting all month columns and using 'Unpivot Columns' requires manually adding each new month column to the selection when the source data grows; it is less maintainable than Unpivot Other Columns.

options.text: Use 'Transpose' followed by 'Use First Row as Headers'
options.explanation: Transpose followed by 'Use First Row as Headers' would produce a transposed table, not an unpivoted one with Product, Month, Sales columns.

options.text: Use 'Group By' on the Product column and aggregate month values
options.explanation: 'Group By' aggregates rows; it does not restructure columns into rows the way Unpivot does.

questions.stem: A measure [Sales LY] uses CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dates[Date])). When the report is filtered to a single month (March 2024), [Sales LY] returns March 2023 correctly. But when filtered to a custom date range (Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2024), [Sales LY] returns a value the user believes is wrong. Why is this most likely?

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR requires a contiguous full-month or full-quarter range; partial month ranges are not supported
options.explanation: is incorrect. `SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR` works on any set of dates including partial months. It has no restriction to full month or quarter boundaries.

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR shifts any set of dates back by exactly one year; the user's expectation about what 'last year' means for a partial range is likely the source of confusion
options.explanation: is correct. `SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR` returns the same set of dates shifted back exactly one year. For Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2024, it returns Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2023 — which is mathematically correct DAX behavior. If the user expects a full January–December 2023 or some other range, the issue is a mismatch between their expectation and what the function actually does, not a bug in the function.

options.text: The Dates table is missing rows for February 2023, causing the calculation to return blank instead of the expected value
options.explanation: is incorrect. A missing row in the Dates table would produce a blank only for that specific missing date. A systematic missing-February problem would affect other periods as well and is an unlikely cause of a plausible-looking but 'wrong' value.

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR works only with standard periods such as full months, quarters, or years; use DATEADD with -1 YEAR for custom ranges
options.explanation: is incorrect. `SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR` is not limited to full standard periods. `DATEADD(Dates[Date], -1, YEAR)` produces the same result as `SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR` for the same reason — it also shifts the filter back one year.

questions.stem: A Power Query table has a column named [Sales Amount] that contains text values like '$1,234.56'. You must convert it to a decimal number. What is the correct sequence of transformations?

options.text: Add a custom column using Number.FromText([Sales Amount])
options.explanation: Number.FromText('[Sales Amount]') also fails when the string contains '$' and ',' characters; the string must be cleaned first.

options.text: Change the column data type directly to Decimal Number
options.explanation: Power Query cannot automatically parse '$1,234.56' as a Decimal Number; the currency symbol and thousands separator cause a type conversion error.

options.text: Use Text.ToNumber in a new custom column
options.explanation: Text.ToNumber does not exist as a Power Query M function; the correct function is Number.FromText, and even that requires a clean numeric string.

options.text: Use Replace Values to remove '$' and ',', then change data type to Decimal Number
options.explanation: Directly changing a text column with currency symbols and thousand separators to Decimal Number causes conversion errors. The correct approach is to first clean the text: Replace Values to remove '$', then Replace Values to remove ',', and finally change the data type to Decimal Number.

questions.reference: [Use Power Query to transform data - Power Query](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-query/power-query-ui)

questions.stem: A Power Query step transforms a column using a formula that references a column that was renamed in a later step. The query throws an error. What is the root cause?

options.text: Power Query does not support renaming columns that are referenced in formulas
options.explanation: Power Query does support renaming columns used in formulas; the issue is step ordering, not a feature restriction.

options.text: The formula references a column that was renamed, so it cannot find the original name
options.explanation: Power Query evaluates each step sequentially. If a rename step is inserted before an existing formula step that uses the old column name, the formula step can no longer find the column by its original name. The column name in the formula must match the name at that point in the step sequence.

options.text: Power Query formulas must use column index positions, not names
options.explanation: Power Query M formulas reference columns by name (e.g., [ColumnName]), not by index position.

options.text: Power Query steps are evaluated top-to-bottom; the column is renamed in a later step, so at the time of the formula step the original name is valid — renaming later causes downstream steps to fail
options.explanation: is a valid description but is less direct than B — the key point is that the formula cannot find the original name because a rename occurred before this step in the sequence.

questions.reference: [Use Power Query to transform data - Power Query](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-query/power-query-ui)

questions.stem: You must combine data from 12 monthly Excel files in a folder into a single table. Each file has the same schema. Which Power Query approach is most maintainable as new monthly files are added?

options.text: Use a Power Query loop to iterate over file names
options.explanation: Power Query has no loop construct; iteration over files is handled by the folder connector's combine function.

options.text: Manually import each file and append the 12 queries
options.explanation: Manually importing each file and appending queries requires adding a new query each month, which is not maintainable.

options.text: Create an ODBC connection to the folder
options.explanation: An ODBC connection to a folder is not a standard Power Query connector type for Excel files.

options.text: Use 'Get Data > Folder', combine files using the combine function, which automatically processes all files in the folder
options.explanation: The Power Query folder connector (Get Data > Folder) lists all files in a directory and applies a combine function to merge them. When new monthly files are added to the folder, refreshing the query automatically includes the new files — no query changes needed.

questions.stem: A dataset refreshes from an on-premises SQL Server database. The refresh fails with 'Unable to connect to the data source'. The on-premises data gateway is installed and running. Which action should you check first?

options.text: Reinstall Power BI Desktop to clear gateway credentials
options.explanation: Tableau Desktop credentials are not involved in gateway-based refreshes; gateway credentials are managed in the Power BI service data source settings, not in Power BI Desktop.

options.text: Upgrade to a Premium capacity to enable on-premises refresh
options.explanation: Premium capacity is not required for on-premises data gateway refresh; it works with Pro licenses.

options.text: Verify that the gateway machine has outbound access to the Power BI service endpoints on port 443
options.explanation: The on-premises data gateway communicates with Power BI service via outbound HTTPS (port 443) and optionally Azure Service Bus (port 5671/5672). If the gateway is running but refresh fails, the most common cause is a firewall blocking outbound connectivity from the gateway machine to Power BI service endpoints.

options.text: Switch the dataset from Import to DirectQuery mode
options.explanation: Switching to DirectQuery mode changes the data architecture entirely and would still require the gateway to connect to the on-premises database.

questions.stem: You must show the percentage contribution of each product to total sales in a bar chart, where the total always represents 100% of currently filtered data. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Use DIVIDE([Product Sales], SUM(Sales[Amount]))
options.explanation: DIVIDE([Product Sales], SUM(Sales[Amount])) in a per-product context returns 100% for every row since SUM(Sales[Amount]) equals [Product Sales] at the product granularity.

options.text: Use DIVIDE([Product Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALLSELECTED(Products)))
options.explanation: ALLSELECTED(Products) removes filters from the Products table while preserving any external slicer/filter context. This means the denominator reflects the total sales of all currently visible products (respecting slicers outside the visual) rather than all products in the dataset.

options.text: Use DIVIDE([Product Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALL(Products)))
options.explanation: ALL(Products) always sums all products regardless of slicers, so the denominator does not change when users filter by region or category.

options.text: Change the bar chart Y-axis to percentage format
options.explanation: Changing the Y-axis to percentage format only changes the display format of the existing measure values; it does not compute a share-of-total.
